coredata.dc:title: Microfluidic paper-based analytical aptasensor for fluorometric β-lactoglobulin determination
coredata.dc:creator: Ortiz-Gómez, Inmaculada
Ipatov, Andrey
Barreiro-Docío, Elisa
Salinas-Castillo, Alfonso
de Orbe-Payá, Ignacio
Fermín Capitán-Vallvey, Luis
Prado, Marta
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we present a new luminescent-based analytical method for sensitive fluorometric determination of a milk allergen, β-lactoglobulin (β-LG), in food products based on a portable, low-cost, and easy-to-use microfluidic paper-based analytical device (µPAD). For this purpose, the developed µPAD consisted on a β-LG specific aptamer immobilized on a cellulose disk, and fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) bioconjugated with its complementary oligonucleotide (DNA), which worked as a label for the aptamer hybridization and its complementary DNA (CQDs-DNA). In a sample with β-LG, the aptamer preferentially binds to β-LG protein and this leads to release of the CQDs-DNA. After washing the µPAD, where the aptamer is immobilized, detection is achieved by fluorescence intensity decrease. An estimation of β-LG concentration can be calculated thanks to the fluorescence changes, for that, a photo of the µPAD is taken using either a digital camera or a smartphone. The µPAD showed an excellent detection response for β-LG in the range of 1.8 to 500 ng/mL with a limit of detection of up to 0.6 ng/mL using the digital camera. Furthermore, the developed method was successfully applied to the determination of β-LG in food samples such as skimmed milk, crispy rice, cookies and chocolate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: µPAD
Aptamer
Carbon Quantum dots
β-LG
Milk allergen
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Complementary hybridization between CQDs-DNA and aptamers
Construction of portable setup for smartphone readout
Preparation of food samples for β-LG determination
Characterization of CQDs bioconjugated with DNA (CQDs-DNA)
Characterization of the functionalized cellulose disks

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministerio de Universidades – Gobierno de España

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the support from the TITAN project funded from the European Union’s HORIZON EUROPE research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 101060739. Additionally, the authors are grateful to the Ministerio de Universidades – Gobierno de España and European Union – NextGenerationEU for the Margarita Salas Postdoctoral fellowship granted to Inmaculada Ortiz-Gómez, within the framework of Royal Decree 289/2021.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Scheme of the µPAD device showing the three areas for analysis and B) PMMA housing made for the µPAD. C) Scheme of the sample flow through the µPAD device.

Workflow of functionalization steps of cellulose disk for the fluorometric recognition of β-LG: A) cellulose oxidation; B) covalent aptamer immobilization; C) hybridation step between CQDs-DNA and aptamer; D) detection of β-LG.
Files of A) PMMA housing of the µPAD, B) dark box pieces for smartphone (dimension are given in mm) and C) photographs of the portable system and the screen of smartphone.
Workflow of the methodology for the determination of β-Lactoglobulin in real samples.

Analytical performance of β-LG determination. A) Change of gray scale coordinate with the variation of β-LG concentration. Min value represented the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-DNA and Max value represented the back-ground. B) Calibration curve for β-LG standard solutions. The data are an average values of three different µPADs.
A) Selectivity assessment of the proposed method for β-LG.
Detection results and recovery rate of β-LG spiked in food products.

head.ce:title: Microfluidic paper-based analytical aptasensor for fluorometric β-lactoglobulin determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Iberian Nanotechnology Laboratory, Av. Mestre José Veiga s/n, 4715-330 Braga, Portugal
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Iberian Nanotechnology Laboratory, Av. Mestre José Veiga s/n, 4715-330 Braga, Portugal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Campus Fuentenueva, Faculty of Sciences, University of Granada, 18071 Granada, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Campus Fuentenueva, Faculty of Sciences, University of Granada, 18071 Granada, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Food Hygiene, Inspection and Control Laboratory, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Nutrition and Bromatology, Campus Terra, University of Santiago de Compostela, 27002 Santiago de Compostela, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Food Hygiene, Inspection and Control Laboratory
Department of Analytical Chemistry, Nutrition and Bromatology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Food Hygiene, Inspection and Control Laboratory, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Nutrition and Bromatology, Campus Terra, University of Santiago de Compostela, 27002 Santiago de Compostela, Spain

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: International Iberian Nanotechnology Laboratory, Av. Mestre José Veiga s/n, 4715-330 Braga, Portugal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we present a new luminescent-based analytical method for sensitive fluorometric determination of a milk allergen, β-lactoglobulin (β-LG), in food products based on a portable, low-cost, and easy-to-use microfluidic paper-based analytical device (µPAD). For this purpose, the developed µPAD consisted on a β-LG specific aptamer immobilized on a cellulose disk, and fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) bioconjugated with its complementary oligonucleotide (DNA), which worked as a label for the aptamer hybridization and its complementary DNA (CQDs-DNA). In a sample with β-LG, the aptamer preferentially binds to β-LG protein and this leads to release of the CQDs-DNA. After washing the µPAD, where the aptamer is immobilized, detection is achieved by fluorescence intensity decrease. An estimation of β-LG concentration can be calculated thanks to the fluorescence changes, for that, a photo of the µPAD is taken using either a digital camera or a smartphone. The µPAD showed an excellent detection response for β-LG in the range of 1.8 to 500 ng/mL with a limit of detection of up to 0.6 ng/mL using the digital camera. Furthermore, the developed method was successfully applied to the determination of β-LG in food samples such as skimmed milk, crispy rice, cookies and chocolate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, ensuring food safety is an issue of major concern for the food industry. Undoubtedly, the availability of reliable, but faster and cheaper analytical methods to detect cross-contamination in food products would help to reduce the social and health burden of food safety issues. One of the key challenges to guarantee a high level of consumer protection is the availability of sensitive and reliable analytical methods to identify specific hazards associated to food before they become a health problem [1]. In this line, the presence of traces of allergens due to cross-contamination during food processing poses an important risk to sensitized individuals. Each year, millions of people get sick, and some even die as a results of the ingestion of allergenic ingredients [2].

β-lactoglobulin (β-LG) has been described as the main allergenic whey protein in cow’s milk which promote allergic reactions even at trace concentrations. β-LG is found at concentrations ranging from 2 to 3 g/L which represents 7–9 % of the total protein content. It is associated with the lipocalin family, playing an important role in the transportation of small hydrophobic molecules such as cholesterol and vitamin D2 [3]. In addition, β-LG can form long stiff fibers under continuous heating, low ionic strength or acid pH. This property of β-LG enables preserving the structural integrity after digestion that allows its absorption via intestinal mucosa as well as further presentation to immunocompetent cells with high allergenic potential [4]. Thus, there is a need to develop new analytical tools for the on-site detection of residues of milk protein allowing for simple, fast, portable and reliable qualitative and quantitative detection of β-LG traces [5].

Many analytical methods have been developed for the detection of food allergens, being the most used the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), polymerase chain reaction (PCR), and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [3,6–12]. These methods are highly sensitive and selective for allergens detection in food. However, the detection and quantification results too complex and expensive since a tedious sample preparation is needed, additionally specific equipment and highly-trained personnel are required to carry out the analysis in the laboratory. In this sense, new analytical methodologies are being implemented to improve certain aspects of traditional techniques. Among these analytical methods, fluorescent assays are presented as promising analytical tools with wide applicability in this field due to their intuitive interpretation of the results, high portability, simple use, low cost and fast response [13–15].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have been highlighted as an interesting advanced nanomaterial to fabricate luminescent probes on the basis of their inherent properties: shape, size, high luminescence and strong photostability, along with the easy modification of its surface [16]. Nevertheless, the development of fluorescence probes is limited due to the lack of selectivity despite owning a good optical performance. In order to overcome this drawback, biomolecules (as aptamers or antibodies) have been proposed as selective ligands [17–20].

Aptamers are artificial receptors (DNA or RNA-based oligonucleotide sequences) which are used as recognition elements for binding to the target [21]. Compared to protein, antibodies or enzymes, aptamers have several advantages for analytical applications due to their high stability, versatility and small size, enabling efficient immobilization, which is important in multiplexed miniaturized systems, low production cost and ease of manufacturing [22]. Further on, aptamers can be simply modified with a high variety of functional groups due to the possibility of reversible denaturation [23]. Because of these advantages, there have been increasing reports on the use of aptamers as molecular recognition elements for sensing applications [24].

However, analytical formats frequently need the immobilization of aptamers to a surface for its integration into a device. An important challenge on aptamers immobilization is to preserve the binding selectivity and affinity that they display in solution [25]. Recently, there has been an increasing development of bioactive paper devices which can be useful to detect different analytes in different fields including food safety [19,26–28]. Contrary to enzymes and antibodies, aptamers may survive dehydration on paper surfaces for paper immobilized biosensing agents. In this line, microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (µPADs) have attracted increasing attention in biosensors, Point of Care (PoC) or Lab on a Chip (LoC) development due to their specific properties such as flexibility and disposability, ease of use, no need for equipment, and portability among others [27]. These advantages make µPADs attractive for food industry. [29]. Consequently, the combination of aptamers with μPADs offers a good alternative for addressing some pressing needs in food analysis such as reduced sample volume and short detection time, improved sensitivity, selectivity, portability, disposability and real-time detection [30].

In this work, we have established a fluorometric method based on the combination of fluorescent carbon quantum dots and an aptamer as recognition element for the detection and quantification of β-LG protein in food products. The µPAD was successfully applied for food evaluation in skimmed milk, crispy rice, cookies and chocolate samples meeting the demands of on-site monitoring in the food safety field. Furthermore, a smartphone was used to capture images which enables the quantitative analyses out of the laboratory.

β-lactoglobulin (β-LG), citric acid monohydrate, phosphate buffered saline (PBS) tablet and N-hydroxysuccinimide 98 % (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), sodium periodate (NaIO4) and sodium cyanoborohydride (NaCNBH3) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (https://www.sigmaaldrich.com) (St. Louis, USA). Agarose electrophoresis grade, from NZYtech, (https://www.NZYtech.com). ELISA kit RIDASCREEN FAST β-lactoglobulin competitive ELISA assay from r-biopharm (Ar. No. R4912), (https://www.r-biopharm.com). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific (https://www.fishersci.es), (Whaltham, Mass., USA). The used reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification step.
The DNA oligonucleotides were acquired from IDT technologies (https://www.idtdna.com) (Coralville, IA, USA). Their base sequences are given as follows:
β-LG aptamer: 5′-NH2-(CH2)6 – CGACGATCGGACCGCAGTACCACCCACCAGC CCCAACATCATGCCCATCCGTGTGTG-3′.
Complementary DNA sequence to part of the β-lactoglobulin aptamer: 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-CACACACGGATGGGCATGATGTTGGGG CT-3′ [31].

Whatman qualitative filter paper grade 1 from Sigma-Aldrich and double sided adhesive tape from Plus Office S.L. were used to fabricate the µPAD. Nylon filter with 0.2 µm pore size from Millipore Millex-GN. All solutions were made using reverse-osmosis type quality water (MilliRO 12 plus MilliQ station from Millipore, conductivity 18.2 MΩ•cm).

The µPAD was designed by Adobe Illustrator software and afterwards was fabricated by craft-cutting technique following the descripted protocol in the bibliography [32]. The device consists of three different cellulose parts or layers prepared individually, as shown in Fig. 1 A inside the red dashed lines. One of these parts is for sampling and leading the sample to the detection zone. The second part is where the aptamer was immobilized to detect β-LG and where the hybridization between CQDs-DNA and aptamer takes place, and the last one is for the transport and waste after β-LG detection. This part of the µPAD included the mentioned waste area which is made using an absorbent pad to collect free CQDs-DNA after β-LG detection. The different areas of the µPAD were connected by two transport channels. Each part of the µPAD were produced on a Whatman grade 1 filter paper sheet. The pattern of µPAD was designed and cut by laser ablation by irradiating the paper with a laser source with controlled speed and power.

To prepare the µPAD for β-LG analysis, a poly(methyl methacrylate) PMMA housing was fabricated by laser engraver with a CO2 laser source in order to connect the different paper layers and protect the paper device, see Fig. 1B. The paper layers were placed onto the box to get the best contact between the three layers once they over-lapped and were correctly aligned, which allows excellent speed and pressure between them. Also, the PMMA housing has a sampling hole (2 mm) and a detection hole (10 mm) to check the fluorescence change after β-LG recognition and washing step. For the µPAD device, a solution of 10 µL of different concentration of β-LG was dropped onto the sampling hole and the sample flowed towards the detection area of the cellulose device by capillarity, as shown in the Fig. 1C.

In this research, the β-LG aptamer described above was employed for the specific detection of β-LG in food products. Therefore, it was necessary to preserve the binding affinity and selectivity of the aptamers while effectively immobilized on the cellulose disk of the µPAD. Thus, the aptamers were immobilized on the cellulose disks as follow: Firstly, 48 cellulose paper disks (10 mm of diameter) were incubated into a Erlenmeyer flask of 250 mL of capacity, with a solution of sodium periodate (NaIO4) 30 mM during 6 h at room temperature by stirring in order to generate aldehyde groups on the cellulose substrate (Fig. 2 A). After the cellulose activation or oxidation step, the cellulose disks were washed twice with MilliQ water at stirring to remove the excess of NaIO4, and dried at room temperature.

In a second step, the activated cellulose disks were incubated into a solution of sodium cyanoborohydride (NaCNBH3) 200 mM containing 40 µL of aptamer 100 µM [25]. Previous to use the aptamer, it was heated for 4 min at 95 °C to dissociate intermolecular interaction. Later, an ice bath was used to cool down to room temperature the aptamer solution. The aptamer immobilization took place using a 24 well plate, the cellulose paper disk were distributed into the well plate one by one in the wells. Then, the cellulose disk and the aptamer solution with NaCNBH3 cited before was shaking during 6 h at room temperature in the dark (Fig. 2B). Then, the cellulose disks were washed twice with MilliQ water at stirring in order to remove the non-covalently bound aptamer. In the end, a blocking step onto the cellulose disks was carried out using an ethanolamine solution 100 µM. For that, 500 µL of ethanolamine solution was added into each well plate with the cellulose paper. In the blocking step the cellulose paper was incubated with ethanolamine for 1 h under steady stirring. Finally, the cellulose disks were washed with phosphate buffer 10 mM at pH 7.4 and were dried at room temperature.

Here, CQDs were synthetized by microwave carbonization of a carbon source, citric acid, as described in detail in the bibliography [33]. Briefly, 1 g of citric acid was dissolved in 4 mL of MilliQ water and 0.5 mL of EDA under continuous stirring. The solution was then placed into a 700 W microwave oven and heated for 60 s. The resultant CQDs were purified by dialysis membrane 1 kDa with 0.01 M HCl overnight.

The CQDs 0.1 mg/mL were mixed with 19 mg of EDC and 22 mg of NHS in phosphate buffer at10 mM and pH 7.4 at room temperature under stirring treatment for 15 min in order to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs for the covalent attachment of the DNA. The amino modified DNA solution was covalently conjugated onto the carboxyl groups of CQDs through EDC/NHS chemistry. Firstly, the DNA solution of 100 µM was heated at 95 °C for 4 min, cooled on ice during 4 min and left at room temperature for a while to facilitate its folding which is responsible for specific binding [34]. Then, 10 µL of the above pretreated DNA solution was added into the activated CQDs solution to conduct the condensation reaction between the carboxyl and amino groups overnight with steady stirring at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Complementary hybridization between CQDs-DNA and aptamers
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose disks with immobilized aptamers were incubated in the CQDs-DNA solution for 120 min at 37 °C under stirring (Fig. 2C). Each cellulose disk was treated individually. For this, a 48 wells microplate was used. The cellulose disks were placed in each well and 100 µL of CQDs-DNA (5 µg/mL) was added. After hybridization, the cellulose disks were washed twice with MilliQ water and PBS 10 mM at pH 7.4 in order to remove the free CQDs-DNA.

10 µL of different concentration of β-LG solutions were added to the sampling zone of the µPAD. After a 20 min incubation at room temperature, 20 µL of phosphate buffer 10 mM pH 7.4 was used to clean the detection zone of the µPAD in order to remove the free CQDs-DNA (Fig. 2D). The µPAD was dried after 20 min and the fluorescence intensity was recorded by taking a photo of the detection area of the µPAD under excitation of 365 nm using a BioRAD Gel DocTM EZ image system with an UV tray (Biorad, Hercules, Ca, USA). The equipment has a digital camera and a transilluminator with a UV light filter. ImageJ 2.0 software was used to analyze the region of interest (ROI) of the image, 5 mm of the detection area of the µPAD. The software converts each region of interest into the gray scale space and this value is denoted by gray scale. An average of the gray scale coordinates from the pixels considered was calculated and used for the determination of β-LG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of portable setup for smartphone readout
ce:section.ce:para: To facilitate the read out of the quantitative results obtained on the µPAD with the smartphone, a portable dark box was designed (Fig. 3 .) with ArtCAM JewelSmith 2011 Software, and a support was manufactured in PMMA using a laser cutter (Wildlaser LS1390plus, Portugal). The Fig. 3A and 3B shows the dimension of the PMMA housing for the µPAD and the portable dark box for the measurement of fluorescence. Herein, the portable attachment developed for the smartphone measurement integrates a UV LED which emits at 365 nm (EOLD-365–525 from OSA Opto Light, Berlin, Germany) at an angle of ∼ 15° with respect to the detection area of the µPAD. The UV LED is powered by a battery and the box has a switch in the bottom. The µPAD incorporated into the PMMA housing is placed in a spline in the middle of the box. The distance between the smartphone and the µPAD was calculated so that the photo of the detection area could be done with a good focus of the camera, (Fig. 3C). The distance between the µPAD and the smartphone was calculated taking into account the camera focus of three different smartphones.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of food samples for β-LG determination
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the developed assay, four different food products including skimmed milk, crispy rice, cookies and chocolate purchased from a local supermarket were analyzed.

Firstly, the food samples were analyzed by a commercial ELISA kit from r-biopharm (RIDASCREEN FAST β-Lactoglobulin competitive ELISA assay) in order to confirm that these solid foods were milk free as it was declared in the labelling. The selected ELISA kit is a competitive enzyme immunoassay for the quantitative analysis of native and processed β-lactoglobulin in rice crispies, chocolate and sausages.

Also, the skimmed milk was analyzed to know the β-LG content. The obtained results confirmed the absence of milk as seen in Table 2. Secondly, the chosen samples, with the exception of milk, were contaminated with milk in order to simulate a possible situation of cross contamination in food industry. The ratio of food sample and milk were calculated to obtain β-LG concentrations of 6.5 ng/mL and 320 ng/mL. According with this, the food samples were contaminated with 100 µL of 1:6500 and 1:320000 milk dilutions. After a simple pretreatment in order to eliminate potential matrix effect to detect β-LG (see Fig. S10), the samples were analyzed with the ELISA kit and the developed µPAD, see Fig. 4 .

An UV–Vis spectrophotometer Shimadzu UV-2550 was used to measure the UV–Vis absorption of the CQDs. The UV–Vis spectrum of the as-prepared CQDs given in Fig. S1A shows a peak at around 370 nm attributable to the π-π* transition of CO and CC bonds (Y. [35]. The fluorescence emission measurements were performed using spectrofluorometer Horiba FluoroMax 4. A bright blue color fluorescence emission with a peak at 460 nm was observed upon excitation of the CQDs solution at 365 nm, see Fig. S1A. Also, the luminescence quantum yield of the CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as the standard. Thus, the quantum yield found for synthetized CQDs was found to be 48 %, see electronic supporting information.

To know the distribution and size of the CQDs, high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, TEM FEI TITAN G2) was used. The included TEM image in the Fig. S1B shows an uniform distribution of the CQDs which present a spherical shape with an average size of 3 nm, as shown in Fig. S1C. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (4000–400 cm−1) was measured using Bruker Vertex 80v vacuum Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Bruker, Billerica, MA, U.S.A). AT-FT-IR spectrum demonstrated the amino and carboxyl bonds formed on the surface of the CQDs, Fig. S1D. Absorption bands at 3016 cm−1 and 2360 cm−1 indicate vibrations of N-H group and CO group, respectively. Peak at 1560 cm−1 corresponds to C-N stretching vibrations. The absorption peak at 1388 cm−1 is originated from the O-H stretching vibration [35–36]. Also, the hydrodynamic size of the CQDs was studied by Dynamic Light Scattering System (DLS) Horiba SZ-100Z as shown in Fig. S2. DLS also showed that the CQDs has a hydrodynamic diameter of an average of 4.3 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs bioconjugated with DNA (CQDs-DNA)
ce:section.ce:para: The bioconjugated CQDs with complementary DNA to β-LG aptamer showed a blue-shifted absorption peak at 350 nm compared to the original maximum absorption wavelength of 370 nm for the CQDs as shown in the Fig. S3A. This shift in the spectrum of CQDs-DNA is an evidence of surface biomodification [37].

Also, two agarose gel electrophoresis were used to demonstrate the successful conjugation between the CQDs and the single-stranded DNA. As shown in the Fig. S3B DNA (lines a1-a3) migrated faster than CQDs-DNA (lines a4-a6) due to the increase of mass to charge ratio. This results also proves the successful conjugation of DNA to CQDs [38]. Due to the small size of the CQDs (∼3 nm) and that the DNA is a single strand of 29 pb, the mobility was analyzed using three different lines in this gel. In the same way, the electrophoresis mobility of DNA labeled with fluorescent CQDs (lines b1 and b2) is slower than CQDs (lines b3 and b4) because of the increase of the overall size and charge after bioconjugation. Thus, these results indicate that the DNA strands were covalently linked to the CQDs.

Likewise, Circular Dichroism (CD) was employed to evaluate the binding of DNA and CQDs after bioconjugation. CD spectra were collected by J-815 Circular Dichroism Spectropolarimeter (Jasco International Co., Tokyo, Japan) under nitrogen atmosphere, provided with quartz cuvette with 10.0 mm path length at a scanning speed of 50 nm/min. Three scans were averaged to get the samples spectra in phosphate buffer 10 mM pH 7.4. For the background spectra, PBS buffer 10 mM pH 7.4 was employed, the volume of each experiment was 2500 µL. The circular dichroism results showed the successful bioconjugation of the CQDs. As presented in the Fig. S3C the unmodified CQDs present a positive peak at 240 nm and the single strand of DNA presents two peak at 240 nm (negative) and 260 nm (positive). However, after the bioconjugation of CQDs with the DNA, two typical bands at 245 nm (negative) and 265 nm (positive) are observed. Thus, the spectral differences between the CQDs, DNA, and the bioconjugated CQDs-DNA showed the changes in the helix structures of the oligomers in each step [39–41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the functionalized cellulose disks
ce:section.ce:para: ATR-FT-IR spectroscopy results demonstrated the structural changes of the cellulose disks during the covalent immobilization of aptamers onto the paper. Thus, the Fig. S4, shows the FT-IR spectrum of cellulose disk before (orange line), after reaction with NaIO4 (blue line) and the aptamer immobilization (green line). The presence of absorption peak at 1733 cm−1 in the spectrum after aldehyde modification is attributed to the CO vibration [25]. This absorption peak is not present in unmodified cellulose paper disk. Also, after immobilization of the aptamer onto cellulose disk, characteristic peaks of aptamer at 1450 cm−1 (corresponding to C-C stretch in ring), 1230 cm−1 and 920 cm−1 corresponding to PO and POH groups, and also a new absorption peak at 1650 cm−1 which is attributed to the N-H typical bending bands of amino group, were observed [25].

Furthermore, an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) surface analysis of the paper disks was carried out to verify the functionalization steps, as seen in Fig. S5. The XPS spectrums were obtained using X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy System ESCALAB 250Xi from Thermo Scientific. As expected, the data for the elemental composition indicate the presence of carbon and oxygen atoms for unmodified cellulose paper disk. The high resolution spectrum of these elements are included in the Fig. S6.The deconvoluted high-resolution C (1 s) spectrum of cellulose shows three main peaks: a peak at 285.2 eV corresponding to C-C bonds, one major peak at 286.4 eV corresponding to C-O bond, and a third one around 287.7 eV that corresponding to CC bonds. Also, the deconvoluted high-resolution O (1 s) spectrum of unmodified cellulose shows two peaks at 534 eV and 532.8 eV corresponding to COC and –OH bonds, respectively [33]. After covalent immobilization of the aptamer, the appearance of a nitrogen at 397 eV (N 1 s) peaks in the XPS spectrum indicates that the aptamer is successfully immobilized onto the paper surface. The deconvoluted high-resolution N (1 s) spectrum shows two peaks at 401.8 eV and 399.7 eV corresponding to C-NH3 + and C-NH2, respectively. In addition, the deconvoluted high-resolution C (1 s) spectrum shows CC, CO, –C(O)–NH– and CC peaks at 288 eV, 287.5 eV, 286 eV and 284.7 eV, respectively. The Table S1. includes the corresponding percentages 61.91 %, 37.75 % and 0.35 % for C1s at 286 eV, O 1 s at 532 eV and N 1 s at 397 eV, respectively.

To achieve a high performance, the following parameters were investigated to improve the fluorescence response of the µPAD: (A) hybridization temperature; (B) hybridization time; (C) concentration of CQDs-DNA; (D) pH effect; (E) volume of sample and (F) reaction time. Respective experimental results and data are given in the supporting information and was summarized in the Fig. S7 and Fig. S8.

For the β-LG detection, β-LG standard solutions were prepared at concentrations ranging from 5 to 800 ng/mL. Subsequently, 10 µL of each standard solution was added to the sampling area of the µPAD. After 20 min, 20 µL of phosphate buffer 10 mM pH 7.4 is added to the µPAD to wash the detection area after β-LG recognition so, the free CQDs-DNA are moved to the waste. The analysis consisted of examining the fluorescence intensity change of the detection area of the µPAD expressed in the gray scale to establish a correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the β-LG concentration after analyzed a photograph of the µPAD, Fig. 5 A.

Here, we have studied the fluorescence intensity change of the µPAD using as detector for image capture, a digital camera included in the Bio-RAD Gel DocTM EZ image system. The quantification was performed by comparing the fluorescence intensity expressed as color in the gray scale by processing the photo in ImageJ software to determine the intensity of color versus the concentrations of β-LG.

The regression equation is y = 156.7–23.3x with a correlation coefficient of 0.985, as seen Fig. 5B. The experimental data shown in the Fig. 5B are an average of three different µPADs for each concentration of β-LG. In light of the 3σ/slope laws, the detection limit of β-LG was calculated to be as low as 0.6 µM, showing a high sensitivity for β-LG. The analytical parameters are shown in the Table 1 .

The stability of the developed µPADs for β-LG were evaluated considering its potential use out of the lab. The µPADs were protected from the light using an aluminum foil and were stored at 4 °C for five weeks. The stability of µPADs were tested each week by reaction with 100 ng/mL of β-LG. The sensitivity of the β-LG remained relatively constant during four weeks. However, after twenty eight days, there was a decrease in sensitivity.

Also, efficiency of hybridation between CQDs-DNA and the immobilized aptamer onto the cellulose disks was evaluated based on the fluorescence measurement. In order to do so, the hybridation protocol between CQDs-DNA and aptamers was evaluated using 18 cellulose disks. The efficiency of hybridation were expressed as percentage relative standard deviation (%RSD), see Table 1. The RSD% for repeatability was calculated based on 18 µPADs prepared under identical conditions on the same day. The processes of aptamer immobilization onto cellulose paper disks, hybridization of CQDs-DNA and aptamers, and fluorescence measurement after adding β-LG at 100 ng/mL were all carried out, resulting in a 5.3 % RSD. Conversely, the RSD% for reproducibility was calculated based on 18 µPADs prepared on different days. Thus, the processes of aptamer immobilization onto cellulose paper disks, hybridization between CQDs-DNA and aptamers, and fluorescence measurement after adding 100 ng/mL of β-LG were performed on three different days, yielding an 8.3 % RSD. These relative standard deviation data demonstrate the good precision of the developed method for β-LG.

To improve the portability of our fluorescent assay for β-LG quantification in food samples, we fabricated a dark portable housing for smartphones and the µPAD, see Fig. 3. The portable system was used for the color analysis of different concentrations of β-LG. The results are included in Fig. S9A, S9B and S9C. Three smartphones with different resolutions cameras (Samsung A72, Samsung S8 and Iphone mini 12) were tested in order to check the accuracy of the detection results. The results showed in the Fig. S9C demonstrated the good accuracy of the proposed method. The lineal range here is from 5 ng/mL to 500 ng/mL. The regression equations calculated for the smartphones were y = 183.9–25.1x with a correlation coefficient (r2) of 0.968 obtained by Samsung A72, y = 183.9 – 24.3x with r2 = 0.978 obtained by Samsung S8 and y = 166.53–21.8x with r2 = 0.983 obtained by IPhone mini 12.

Furthermore, the proposed method has been compared with other reported strategies using aptamers as recognition element, including cellulose paper substrate for detecting β-LG or fluorescent nanoparticles as labels to provide an optical signal. The cellulose platforms for the colorimetric and fluorescent determination of β-LG in food samples included in the Table S2 present common characteristic of paper-based devices: low-cost, fast reaction time, low volume of sample and allowing multiplexing, among others. For example, [42] developed a µPAD to determine β-LG in milk samples using gold nanoparticles conjugated with biotinylated aptamers. The specific colorimetric detection of β-LG in this work presents a lineal range from 460 to 18400 ng/mL with a LOD of 143.5 ng/mL. The lateral flow test developed by [43] is presented as a simple, rapid and multiplex detection method for colorimetric determination of allergens in milk with a lineal range from 588.8 to 9200 ng/mL and with a LOD of 920 ng/mL. This method uses an aptamer as recognition element and gold nanoparticles to obtain the colorimetric response. Also, [44] developed a paper sensor chip using aptamer marked with fluorescent quantum dots and grapheme oxide to detect β-LG in different food samples: infant formula, rice porridge and cookies. The detection platform developed in this work enables a visual detection of β-LG in a range of 390 to 1000000 ng/mL. In this work either a digital camera or a smartphone can be used to take a photo of the paper and estimate the allergen concentration in each sample.

On the other hand, the fluorescence based methods in solution presented in the Table S2 showed better analytical parameters (as lineal range or detection limit) compared with the descripted paper-based devices due to the higher robustness of these methodologies since they use fluorescence spectrophotometer to obtain the analytical information. For example, [34] used carbon dots as a label for the complementary oligonucleotide DNA to aptamer immobilized on magnetite nanoparticles. The lineal range here is from 0.25 to 50 ng/mL with a detection limit of 0.037 ng/mL. [45] used WS2 nanosheets and fluorescent dye FAM-labeled β-lactoglobulin aptamer. The fluorescence method shown here have a LOD of 20.4 ng/mL. Also, [46] employed quantum dot aptamer graphene oxide (QD-Apt-GO) to determine β-lactoglobulin from 360 to 500000 ng/mL with a detection limit of 96 ng/mL in cookies, rice, and infant formula samples.

Thus, the methodology presented in our work for the fluorescent β-LG determination using carbon quantum dots as label for complementary DNA to the immobilized aptamer onto cellulose paper, shows a detection limit of 0.60 ng/mL and a lineal range from 1.81 to 500 ng/mL. The proposed method shows higher sensitivity than reported paper-based devices developed for the β-LG detection. This enhanced analytical sensitivity is attributed to the covalent aptamer immobilization on paper. The paper substrate has a great superficial area for aptamer immobilization providing sufficient space to fold encountering no steric interferences, guarantying as well a high binding capacity for the aptamer target. In addition to this, in our work the aptamer was hybridized with a second complementary DNA sequence marked with fluorescent carbon quantum dots nanoparticles. Subsequent to target binding, complementary DNA was released and then became available for interaction with β-LG.

In addition, the developed µPAD was compared with the current conventional commercial assays for β-LG available on the market according to their analytical performance and technical specifications, see Table S3. Compared with the commercial assays for β-LG, the main advantages of the developed µPAD are its ease of use, portability, since not bulky equipment is needed, and the sensitivity of the assay, since the estimated LOD is lower than that of commercial kits. The µPAD is disposable and does not generate liquid residues because the washing step is incorporated onto the device. Our developed µPAD provides a fast response alternative to the commercial methods. The reaction time is shorter than most of the commercial assays, just 40 min; 20 min for β-LG recognition and 20 min for the washing and drying step, and it requires an smaller volume of sample, as little as 10 µL. Besides, this paper-based assay enables qualitative and quantitative results because the fluorescence change in the device can be related with the concentration of β-LG by taking a photo of the µPAD using a smartphone and analyzing the results as described above.

The response of β-LG, BSA, casein, γ-globulin, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, ascorbic acid, glucose, galactose, mannose and fructose were investigated for interference by the proposed method. The study was carried out by triplicate, the vertical bars included in the Fig. 6 . represent the standard deviation for three different paper devices. As shown in Fig. 6. it is evident that the fluorescence signals to all studied species are negligible at 80 ng/mL. The obtained results demonstrated the good selectivity of the proposed method to β-LG.

To demonstrate the applicability of the µPAD, we carried out the β-LG determination in skimmed milk, as positive control, crispy rice, cookies and chocolate samples. The solid samples were free of milk content and were deliberately spiked with known amounts of skimmed milk to imitate the cross contamination that could happen during food manufacturing. The protocol indicated by the manufacturer and detailed in the supporting information 8. Pretreatment protocol of β-LG extraction from real samples. After a simple pre-treatment, see Fig. S10 the resulting extracts were tested in parallel with the developed µPAD, and the ELISA kit. There was no β-LG be found in samples of rice, cookies and chocolate spiked with 6.5 ng/mL of β-LG by commercial ELISA kit because of the detection range of it was 167 – 4500 ng/mL. For other samples the results of the proposed µPAD were in agreement with ELISA analysis, see Table 2 . The correlation between the µPAD and commercial ELISA kit was not less than 89.4 %, indicating that the proposed µPAD was reliable, with a low detection limit and had good potential application for real food samples. The average recoveries of the spiked β-LG ranged from 89.4 to 103.3 %. For all tested samples, three replicates of each experiment were performed and each experiment were performed three times for all samples.

In summary, we report a simple and effective strategy for determination of milk traces in food through the detection of β-lactoglobulin. Thus, µPAD aptasensor proposed here is presented as a low cost, portable, instrument-free, and highly sensitive platform for fluorescent determination of β-lactoglobulin with an easy pretreatment of sample. Combined with the µPAD aptasensor, a smartphone sensing platform integrated with a UV LED and dark housing has been developed for quantitative determination of β-LG via a fluorescence change response, enabling its use on decentralized settings without the need of extensive training. As presented here, the developed µPAD, enables the detection of β-LG with a limit of detection of 0.6 ng/mL which has proven to be clearly superior to previously developed paper based devices and the tested commercial kit. According to the perspectives of the developed µPAD, this device could be of great value and high interest to the food industry, providing a necessary, as well as more direct, simpler, portable and low-cost tool to carry out the determination of allergens in food samples, to control their entire supply chains, particularly the risk of cross contamination due to its superior sensitivity.

Inmaculada Ortiz-Gómez is a recipient of a Margarita Salas Postdoctoral fellowship funded by Ministerio de Universidades – Gobierno de España and the European Union – NextGenerationEU, within the framework of Royal Decree 289/2021. This work was supported by the TITAN project funded from the European Union’s HORIZON EUROPE research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 101060739.

Inmaculada Ortiz-Gómez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Andrey Ipatov: Software. Elisa Barreiro-Docío: Investigation. Alfonso Salinas-Castillo: Supervision. Ignacio de Orbe-Payá: Supervision. Luis Fermín Capitán-Vallvey: . Marta Prado: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the support from the TITAN project funded from the European Union’s HORIZON EUROPE research and innovation programme under grant agreement No 101060739. Additionally, the authors are grateful to the Ministerio de Universidades – Gobierno de España and European Union – NextGenerationEU for the Margarita Salas Postdoctoral fellowship granted to Inmaculada Ortiz-Gómez, within the framework of Royal Decree 289/2021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110121.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article: Detailed description of characterization of CQDs and CQDs-DNA, cellulose disk, optimization of method, smartphone setup and measurement, comparative assays for β-LG determination and pretreatment protocol of β-LG extraction.
Supplementary data 1

Green fluorescent carbon quantum dots functionalized with polyethyleneimine, and their application to aptamer-based determination of thrombin and ATP.
